perturbation.original_text: (ii) Equifax shall hold the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs) in strict confidence; will not remove or destroy any proprietary markings of the Certegy Group on or contained in the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs); and will include the copyright and patent notices of the licensor as specified from time to time by the licensor for the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs) on and in all copies of the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs);
perturbation.changed_text: (ii) Equifax shall treat the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs) with reasonable care; will not remove or destroy any proprietary markings of the Certegy Group on or contained in the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs); and will include the copyright and patent notices of the licensor as specified from time to time by the licensor for the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs) on and in all copies of the Licensed Certegy Materials (excluding the Utility Software Programs);
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires Equifax to hold the Licensed Certegy Materials 'in strict confidence,' setting a high standard of care. The modified version replaces 'in strict confidence' with 'with reasonable care,' lowering the required standard. This introduces a contradiction because in the original form strict confidence means a stronger security protocol but in the modified state reasonable care means its more acceptable. This difference in wording could lead to disputes over whether Equifax adequately protected the materials, especially given other sections of the agreement place high confidentiality standards on Company Information, which overlaps in definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
(a) Each party acknowledges, and shall cause each member of the Group to acknowledge, that it is in possession of significant confidential or proprietary information concerning the business, operations and assets of the members of the other Group.
(b) Each party shall, and shall ensure that each member of its Group shall, (i) receive and hold the Company Information of the other Group in trust and in strictest confidence; (ii) protect such Company Information from disclosure and in no event take any action causing, or fail to take the action necessary in order to prevent, any such Company Information to lose its character as Company Information; (iii) exercise at a minimum the same care it would exercise to protect its own highly confidential information; and (iv) not use, reproduce, distribute, disclose, or otherwise disseminate the Company Information of the other Group, (A) except as authorized pursuant to this Agreement or any Related Agreement, or (B) except pursuant to a requirement of a governmental agency or of law without similar restrictions or other protections against public disclosure; provided, however, with respect to disclosures pursuant to (B) above, the receiving party must first give written notice of such required disclosure to the disclosing party, take reasonable steps to allow the disclosing party to seek to protect the confidentiality of the Company Information required to be disclosed, make a reasonable effort to obtain a protective order requiring that the Company Information so disclosed be used only for the purposes for which disclosure is required, and shall disclose only that part of the Company Information which, in the written opinion of its legal counsel, it is required to disclose. In no event shall the receiving party exercise less than a reasonable standard of care to keep confidential the Company Information. Any and all reproductions of such Company Information must prominently contain a confidential legend.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated that Equifax shall hold the Licensed Certegy Materials in "strict confidence." The changed text weakens this obligation to treat the materials "with reasonable care." This creates a direct contradiction with the 'Contradicted Text,' which mandates that each party shall "receive and hold the Company Information of the other Group in trust and in strictest confidence" and "exercise at a minimum the same care it would exercise to protect its own highly confidential information." Reasonable care is a lower standard than strictest confidence, introducing a clear conflict in the level of protection required.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the license rights granted in Section 4.4, as between the parties, the Licensed Certegy Materials shall be and shall remain the sole and exclusive property of the Certegy Group and the members of the Certegy Group may make any internal use and may commercially exploit any enhancements to the Licensed Materials made or caused to be made by members of the Equifax Group, as they shall deem appropriate without any obligation to any member of the Equifax Group or other restriction. The Certegy Group may in particular distribute and manufacture, or cause to be manufactured or distributed by any third party, any such enhancements and/or Licensed Certegy Materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the license rights granted in Section 4.4, as between the parties, the Licensed Certegy Materials shall be and shall remain the sole property of the Certegy Group, without the exclusivity clause and the members of the Certegy Group may make any internal use and may commercially exploit any enhancements to the Licensed Materials made or caused to be made by members of the Equifax Group, as they shall deem appropriate without any obligation to any member of the Equifax Group or other restriction. The Certegy Group may in particular distribute and manufacture, or cause to be manufactured or distributed by any third party, any such enhancements and/or Licensed Certegy Materials.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'exclusive' from sole and exclusive property, means the Certegy Group's right and ownership is not being expressed completely, introducing an element of ambiguity, due to the fact now its not the sole owner. The main effect is if it is or not a sole property, it will have different legal implications for the other party to exercise. This is a clear contradiction to ensure which party has the rights, which could cause disputes and affect the enforceability of the licensing agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (g) Except for the Equifax Group's rights described in Section 4.4(a), (b) and (e) above, the Certegy Group's rights in and to the Licensed Certegy Materials shall be and remain the exclusive property of Certegy or the respective Designated Certegy Member.
perturbation.justification: The original text states "the Licensed Certegy Materials shall be and shall remain the sole and exclusive property of the Certegy Group". The changed text removes the word 'exclusive', stating "the Licensed Certegy Materials shall be and shall remain the sole property of the Certegy Group". The contradicted text states "the Certegy Group's rights in and to the Licensed Certegy Materials shall be and remain the exclusive property of Certegy". By removing 'exclusive', the changed text creates a direct contradiction. The contradicted text specifies 'exclusive property', while the changed text omits this, creating ambiguity about the Certegy Group's property rights. This is a strong contradiction because it directly impacts the enforceability of the exclusivity clause and aligns with the "Omissions – In Text Contradiction" definition because essential language is removed or altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: From time to time, at Equifax's request and without further consideration, Certegy shall, and shall cause each member of the Certegy Group, as applicable, to execute, acknowledge and deliver such documents, instruments or assurances and take such other action as Equifax may reasonably request to more effectively assign, convey and transfer any of the Transferred Certegy Assets and Transferred Certegy Third Party Agreements. Certegy will assist Equifax in the vesting, collection or reduction to possession of such Transferred Certegy Assets and Transferred Certegy Third Party Agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: From time to time, at Equifax's request and subject to a reasonable fee to cover expenses, Certegy shall, and shall cause each member of the Certegy Group, as applicable, to execute, acknowledge and deliver such documents, instruments or assurances and take such other action as Equifax may reasonably request to more effectively assign, convey and transfer any of the Transferred Certegy Assets and Transferred Certegy Third Party Agreements. Certegy will assist Equifax in the vesting, collection or reduction to possession of such Transferred Certegy Assets and Transferred Certegy Third Party Agreements.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that Certegy must provide assistance to Equifax in transferring assets 'without further consideration,' indicating no additional cost to Equifax. By changing this to 'subject to a reasonable fee,' a contradiction is created in Section 10.1, as it implies that Equifax may now be required to pay for this assistance. The term 'reasonable fee' is subjective, and it will generate disputes about what expenses will be covered in this assistance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Except as specifically provided in this Agreement or any Related Agreement, all costs and expenses incurred in connection with the preparation, execution, delivery and implementation of this Agreement and with the consummation of the transactions contemplated by this Agreement (including transfer taxes and the fees and expenses of all counsel, accountants and financial and other advisors) shall be paid by Equifax.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates assistance 'without further consideration,' implying no cost to Equifax. The changed text introduces a 'reasonable fee,' directly contradicting the Contradicted Text, which states Equifax pays all costs except as specifically provided in the agreement. The introduction of a fee where none was previously specified creates a clear contradiction, as Equifax now has an obligation to pay that was not originally present.
